subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs are communicated with clarity and precision (titles, units, significant figures, error bars, regression).
Uncertainties are separated into instrumental and average components and clearly shown in the main graph.
Processing steps (averaging, regression, theoretical comparison) are logically executed and correct.
weaknesses: Key uncertainty analyses are missing (propagation of slope error, frequency calibration uncertainty).
Minor typographical/formatting slip (â4.85.547â) could confuse readers.
Limited significantâfigure discipline in some calculations indicates small inconsistencies.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data is both clear and precise, but omissions in full uncertainty treatment and minor errors in formatting prevent a higher band. The work aligns with a strong performance in the 3â4 range, earning a 4.

strengths: The research question is situated in a clear, specific context (resonance in a waterâfilled PVC pipe linked to flute acoustics).
The methodology is described in sufficient detail (equipment list, set-up photo, numbered steps, trials) to allow reproduction.
Methodological considerations (environmental conditions, material properties, calibration) are not only listed but explained with respect to their impact on data quality.
weaknesses: Minor narrative digressions (personal anecdote) slightly detract from the academic focus.
Some procedural details remain vague (precise waterâlevel adjustment technique, absence of supplier calibration specs for tuning forks).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although there are small ambiguities and a brief narrative detour, the contextual framing is precise, the method is reproducible, and methodological considerations are well explained. These strengths place the work at the top of the rubric band, warranting a full 6.

strengths: Conclusion directly answers the research question and is fully consistent with the analysis (inverse relationship, derived speed of sound).
The result is justified by comparison with the accepted 343 mÂ·sâ»Â¹ and linked to standing-wave theory.
The narrative is coherent and aligns experimental findings with theoretical expectations.
weaknesses: Lacks mention of literature uncertainty ranges for the accepted speed value.
Scientific context could be broadened (e.g., architectural acoustics) for deeper relevance.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is well supported and internally consistent, satisfying the top descriptor for justification, but it stops short of deeper contextual or quantitative comparisons in the literature, placing it at a 5.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identified (temperature fluctuations, fork calibration, waterâlevel adjustment) and their qualitative impact is explained.
Improvements proposed (precise sensors, exploring harmonics, material control) are realistic and relevant to the identified issues.
The evaluation shows clear understanding of how errors arise in the experiment.
weaknesses: Explanations of how each suggested improvement quantitatively reduces error are limited.
No calculation of the expected impact of methodological changes (e.g., error magnitudes per Â°C or mm).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes specific limitations and offers relevant improvements, meeting the criteria for a strong but not maximal band. The absence of quantitative impact analysis confines the score to 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student clearly situates the research question within the specific context of resonance in a water-filled PVC pipe and links it to real-world applications (flute acoustics). The context is precise, relevant and directly frames why the investigation is worthwhile. Minor narrative digressions do not detract from the otherwise appropriate specificity, so placement in the top band is justified, though not at the maximum because the broader significance could be tighter.

Instrumental and average uncertainties are calculated and displayed; error bars appear on the main graph. However, uncertainty in the slope, propagated error in the derived speed of sound, and frequency calibration uncertainty are absent. These omissions mean the treatment is competent but not fully rigorous, matching the 3-4 band with a score of 4.

Relevant methodological considerations (environmental conditions, pipe material, water density, tape precision, calibration of forks) are not only listed but usually accompanied by explanations of why they matter for collecting sufficient data. While quantitative estimates are limited, the discussion goes beyond statements and demonstrates clear understanding, meriting the lower end of the 5-6 band.

Specific weaknessesâtemperature fluctuations, fork calibration, water-level adjustmentâare identified and their potential impacts on data are qualitatively explained, demonstrating more than a generic list. Quantitative evaluation of magnitude is absent, so the work meets the descriptor for 3-4 and earns the upper value.

The step-by-step method (equipment list, set-up photograph, numbered procedure, five trials) allows another investigator to replicate the work, but some details remain vague: how resonance is audibly identified, exact water-level adjustment technique, and room temperature value. These small ambiguities lower reproducibility slightly, fitting best into the 3-4 band; the clearer elements earn the upper value of 4.

Averages, linear regression and theoretical comparisons are correct and logically executed. Minor arithmetic/formatting slips (e.g. â4.85.547â) do not affect numerical outcomes, but lack of error propagation and limited significant-figure discipline indicate some inconsistencies. Hence processing is sound but not flawless, warranting a high score within the 3-4 band.

Improvements such as using precise sensors, exploring higher harmonics, and controlling pipe materials are realistic and relevant, but explanations of how each would specifically reduce the identified errors are limited. This corresponds to the lower end of the 3-4 band, so a score of 3 is awarded.

Raw and processed tables include titles, units, significant figures and uncertainties; graphs are labelled, scaled and include error bars and best-fit equations. Communication is simultaneously clear (easy to follow) and precise (correct notation, explicit Â± values), satisfying the top-band descriptors, though minor typographical slips prevent awarding the maximum 6.

The student compares the experimental speed of sound to the accepted 343 m sâ»Â¹ value and discusses agreement. References to standing-wave theory further anchor results in accepted context. While valid, the discussion is brief and lacks exploration of literature uncertainty ranges, fitting best in the 3-4 band.

The conclusion explicitly answers the research question and is fully consistent with the inverse relationship demonstrated in graphs and calculations. It references the derived speed of sound and aligns observed trends with theoretical expectations. The justification is solid, but depth could be enhanced with quantitative residual analysis, so a 5 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The connection of topic to real-world flute acoustics provides engaging context and clarifies the investigationâs relevance.
Personal anecdote engages reader but adds narrative digression; consider condensing to maintain academic tone.
The research question is clear and focused, but could more strongly articulate its significance in instrument design applications.
Independent variable assumes tuning fork frequencies are exact yet acknowledges no uncertainty; add calibration data or supplier specs.
Dependent variable description is precise, with uncertainty clearly defined; this enhances reproducibility of measurements.
Graph of f versus 1/L is well labeled with error bars and regression line; presentation is both clear and precise.
Controlled variables list is comprehensive but would benefit from specifying measured room temperature and humidity values.
Procedure step to identify resonance is described qualitatively; include quantitative criteria or use a decibel meter to reduce observer bias.
Outline of instrument calibration demonstrates methodological rigor; labeling tuning forks ensures correct assignment during trials.
Raw data table includes consistent significant figures and uncertainties, improving clarity of data recording.
Average uncertainty calculation uses correct formula but omits propagation of slope uncertainty; propagate regression error into final speed value.
Uncertainty components are clearly separated (instrumental vs average); this distinction strengthens the analysis.
Slope extraction statement misformats multiplication (â4.85.547â); correct to $$v=4\times85.547$$ to avoid confusion.
Theoretical comparison uses $$v\approx342.2\,\mathrm{m/s}$$ against accepted 343 m/s, but lacks mention of literature uncertainty range.
Calculated and measured lengths comparison table clearly shows deviations; including percent error would deepen the analysis.
Conclusion restates inverse relationship and links to theoretical model; this justification is coherent and fully consistent with analysis.
Practical applications in instrument design are mentioned, but expanding on other fields (e.g., architectural acoustics) could enrich scientific context.
Evaluation section identifies specific weaknesses (temperature, fork calibration, water adjustment) and comments on their qualitative impact.
Suggested improvement to use precise sensors and explore harmonics is relevant but needs explanation of how it reduces identified errors.
Systematic errors section outlines effects of temperature fluctuation; consider quantifying expected change in $$v$$ per Â°C variance.
Parallax error mitigation is described; noting residual uncertainty contribution would strengthen evaluation.
Random error due to hit consistency is realistic; suggest use of mechanical striker to standardize impact energy.
Instrumental error from tape precision is stated; combine this with calibration uncertainty to calculate total measurement error.
Setup instability error is acknowledged; recommend adding damping material or stiffer clamps to minimize movement.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title: Exploring the effect of varying the frequency of an external sound source on the resonance length in an open-ended resonance tube.

userSubmission.researchQuestion: Exploring the effect of varying the frequency of an external sound source on the resonance length in an open-ended resonance tube.

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.

userSubmission.researchQuestion:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
